tasks.search_intent: Definitionen und Taxonomien von CBM vs reaktiv/korrektiv und präventiv (time/use-based) Instandhaltung finden.
tasks.task_rationale: Liefert belastbare Begriffsabgrenzungen und ein Klassifikationsschema als Basis für den Vergleich.

tasks.search_intent: Kernunterschiede in Prozessen/Methoden: Zustandsüberwachung, Diagnose, Prognose (PHM), Entscheidungslogik gegenüber traditionellen Methoden herausarbeiten.
tasks.task_rationale: Erarbeitet die methodischen Bausteine (Sensorik→Daten→Modelle→Maßnahme) als Differenzierungsmerkmale.

tasks.search_intent: Quantitative Vergleichskennzahlen zwischen CBM und traditionellen Strategien (Kosten, Verfügbarkeit, Ausfallraten, MTBF/MTTR, Risiko) sammeln.
tasks.task_rationale: Stützt den Vergleich durch messbare Effekte und Benchmark-Ergebnisse aus Studien/Industrieanwendungen.

tasks.search_intent: Maritime Leitlinien/Standards/Regelwerke zu CBM und zustandsbasierter Überwachung (IMO/ISM, IACS, Klassifikationsgesellschaften) identifizieren.
tasks.task_rationale: Verortet CBM im maritimen Compliance- und Regelwerkskontext und zeigt akzeptierte Praktiken.

tasks.search_intent: Maritime Fallstudien zu CBM (Antrieb, Hilfsmaschinen, Propeller, Rumpfstruktur) und deren Vergleich zu planmäßiger Wartung finden.
tasks.task_rationale: Ergänzt die Grundlagen durch praxisnahe Beispiele und domänenspezifische Implementierungsvarianten.

research_plan.reasoning: Die Forschungsfrage zielt auf eine begriffliche und methodische Abgrenzung von zustandsorientierter Instandhaltung (Condition-Based Maintenance, CBM) gegenüber herkömmlichen Instandhaltungsstrategien (reaktiv/korrektiv, präventiv/zeit- bzw. nutzungsbasiert, zustandsüberwacht). Für die maritime Domäne sind zusätzlich Klassifikationsgesellschaften, ISM/IMO-Rahmen sowie typische Schiffssysteme (Antrieb, Hilfsmaschinen, Rumpf/Struktur) relevant. Die Zerlegung fokussiert (1) definitorische Grundlagen, (2) Vergleichsrahmen/Taxonomien, (3) messbare Unterschiede (Daten, Sensorik, Prognostik, Entscheidungslogik) und (4) maritime Normen/Guidelines und Fallstudien.

Hülle) e s a h p s g n u z t u n s n e b e L e c n a n e t n i a M & s n o i t a r e p O Echtzeiterfassung während des XT10 gesamten Produktlebenszyklus Windpark Abnutzungsprognose aus DZ Physikalische Parametrierung und Simulation aktiver Systeme mittels DZ Digitale Werft (Wartung) Smart Port: DZ eines Hafens Inbetriebnahme, Außerbetriebnahme Testungen von Aufrüstungen und Reparaturen Die aus dem gleichen Hause stammende Software ShipManager Hull umfasst eine interaktive 3

content.title: Studie Digitaler Zwilling Schiffbau Schiffsbetrieb 2023
content.abstract_or_snippet: bar machen�« für die traditionsreiche Branche mit seinen speziellen Anfor- derungen und Herausforderungen – wie Besonderheiten bei der Datenübertragung auf See, komplexer Stakeholder- und Eignerstruktur oder vergleichsweise langer Produktlebenszyk- len etc. – noch sehr jung. Die im Rahmen dieser Ausarbeitung ausgewerteten Interviews haben einen positiven Ausblick der Branche auf die Poten- tiale der Technologie des Digitalen Zwillings gegeben und es wurden auch für den Aftersales attraktive Kernthemen für die weitere Forschung identifiziert. Voraussetzung ist in diesem Kontext für die Beteiligten, wie bei allen unternehmerischen Entscheidungen, eine absehbare Amortisation der entspre- chenden Anfangsinvestition. Zentrale positive Effekte werden durch die zielgerichtete Nutzung von Datenströmen zur Optimierung des Schiffs- und Flottenmanagements gesehen, die bei konsequenter Umset- zung Flotteneffekte sichtbar und Lösungsansätze empirisch belegbar machen. Innovative Dienstleistungskonzepte, wie etwa das virtuelle Trai- ning von Fachpersonal oder die remoteunterstützte Wartung und Instandhaltung von schwer zugänglichen Systemen, bieten darüber hinaus Potentiale, um den Betrieb der maritimen Anlagen weiter zu optimieren und kostenintensive Reisen zu reduzieren. Nicht zuletzt wird nach Expertenmeinung der Digitale Zwilling im Rahmen der Umweltwende auch instrumental zur Ent- wicklung in Richtung einer nachhaltigen Schifffahrt beitragen können. Es haben sich jedoch auch Hemmfaktoren herauskristallisiert, die einem breiten Einsatz in der Branche entgegenstehen. Angefangen bei einer traditionsbewussten Mentalität vieler Branchenvertreter über eine nicht final geklärte Frage der Datenhoheit bis hin zur schleppenden Netzinfrastruktur bezüg- lich des Datenaustausches auf den Meeren und im Inland, verzögern als technologieunabhängige Faktoren eine breite Akzeptanz des Digitalen Zwillings. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass die teilweise in der vor

Die im Rahmen dieser Ausarbeitung ausgewerteten Interviews haben einen positiven Ausblick der Branche auf die Poten- tiale der Technologie des Digitalen Zwillings gegeben und es wurden auch für den Aftersales attraktive Kernthemen für die weitere Forschung identifiziert. Voraussetzung ist in diesem Kontext für die Beteiligten, wie bei allen unternehmerischen Entscheidungen, eine absehbare Amortisation der entspre- chenden Anfangsinvestition. Zentrale positive Effekte werden durch die zielgerichtete Nutzung von Datenströmen zur Optimierung des Schiffs- und Flottenmanagements gesehen, die bei konsequenter Umset- zung Flotteneffekte sichtbar und Lösungsansätze empirisch belegbar machen. Innovative Dienstleistungskonzepte, wie etwa das virtuelle Trai- ning von Fachpersonal oder die remoteunterstützte Wartung und Instandhaltung von schwer zugänglichen Systemen, bieten darüber hinaus Potentiale, um den Betrieb der maritimen Anlagen weiter zu optimieren und kostenintensive Reisen zu reduzieren. Nicht zuletzt wird nach Expertenmeinung der Digitale Zwilling im Rahmen der Umweltwende auch instrumental zur Ent- wicklung in Richtung einer nachhaltigen Schifffahrt beitragen können. Es haben sich jedoch auch Hemmfaktoren herauskristallisiert, die einem breiten Einsatz in der Branche entgegenstehen. Angefangen bei einer traditionsbewussten Mentalität vieler Branchenvertreter über eine nicht final geklärte Frage der Datenhoheit bis hin zur schleppenden Netzinfrastruktur bezüg- lich des Datenaustausches auf den Meeren und im Inland, verzögern als technologieunabhängige Faktoren eine breite Akzeptanz des Digitalen Zwillings. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass die teilweise in der vor

metadata.task_rationale: Liefert belastbare Begriffsabgrenzungen und ein Klassifikationsschema als Basis für den Vergleich.

content.abstract_or_snippet: ext © Adobe Stock Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Erfolgsfaktoren im Bereich Aftersales-Service Einschätzung durch die Unternehmen N = 51 Servicequalität Optimales Bestandsmanagement Flächendeckendes Serviceangebot Angebote auf Kunden- segmente zuschneiden Geringe Logistikkosten g n u t u e d e B e g i t f n ü k u Z h c o h l e t t i m Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit Reaktions- und Lieferzeiten Minimale Ausfallzeit beim Kunden Produktdesign für Aftersales optimieren Nutzen transparent kommunizieren Modulares Aftersales-Angebot Schlankes Ersatzteilsortiment Sporadische Nachfrage nach Ersatzteilen & Services managen Integration der eigenen Zulieferer mittel hoch Handlungsbedarf Abbildung 5: Erfolgsfaktoren Aftersales [eigene Darstellung nach VDMA] der Produkte mit Datenverkehrsnetzen, die derzeit besonders auf Hoher See noch nicht ausreichend ausgebaut sind, um einen umfassenden Datentransfer ortsunabhängig zu gewährleisten [8]. Diese Handlungsfelder für die Digitalisierung des maritimen Service ergänzen herkömmliche produktbegleitende Dienst- leistungen wie Wartung und Instandhaltung, das Angebot von Ersatzteilen, die Betreuung des Kunden und die Durchführung von Schulungen. Trotz vielfältiger Chancen ist eine Fokussierung auf produkt- begleitende Dienstleistungen allein kein Patentrezept für einen langfristigen Erfolg. Über viele Branchen hinweg ist zu beobachten, dass die Bereitstellung von Aftersales-Services oft eine größere Komplexität innehat als die Herstellung der Primärprodukte selbst. Hinzu kommt, dass in vielen Unter- nehmen produktbegleitende Dienstleistungen eher historisch und aus der Notwendigkeit heraus gewachsen sind, Primär- produkte nach dem Verkauf mit Ersatzteilen und Wartung zu versorgen. 27 © Adobe Stock Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext

content.abstract_or_snippet: Automatisches Erstellen von Gefahrengutdaten für Rettungskräfte Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Digitalisierungsgrad Nutzungspotentiale eines DZ in Standard Bezug auf Kosteneinsparung nicht digital Digitalisierung Digitaler und Effizienz in (CAD / PLM) Zwilling Geschäftsprozessen X X X X X Echtzeitüberwachung von Schlüsselsystemen und Predictive Maintenance Ansätze für die Instandhaltung (X)T2 As-Built-Measurements; Entwicklung übergreifendes DZ Modell Cyber-Physical Twin XT3 Fusion von DZ für Hersteller- Feedback (Abnutzungsverlauf); Lebenslaufanalyse; Modelloptimierung DZ XT4 Remote Maintainance / Lebenslaufanalyse; Modellbildung DZ XT5 As-Built-Measurements / Optimierung übergreifendes DZ Modell / Übergabefreie Dokumentation XT6 Optimierung Prozessabläufe / Remote Support XT11 Spezifizierung und einmalige Zeitaufnahme XT7 Validierung der Entwürfe / Remote Training für veränderte Hardware 31 Teilintegrierte Anwendungsfälle mit großem Digitalisierungs- potential Trimm-, Stabilitäts- und Festigkeitsüberwachung Vernetzte Ver- & Entsorgungsanlagen Überwachung Antriebssysteme Vernetzte Brückensysteme, ECDIS und integrierte Navigationssysteme Überwachen der Integrität (insb. Hülle) e s a h p s g n u z t u n s n e b e L e c n a n e t n i a M & s n o i t a r e p O Echtzeiterfassung während des XT10 gesamten Produktlebenszyklus Windpark Abnutzungsprognose aus DZ Physikalische Parametrierung und Simulation aktiver Systeme mittels DZ Digitale Werft (Wartung) Smart Port: DZ eines Hafens Inbetriebnahme, Außerbetriebnahme Testungen von Aufrüstungen und Reparaturen Die aus dem gleichen Hause stammende Software ShipManager Hull umfasst eine interaktive 3

content.abstract_or_snippet: to guide service system development, as noted by [18]. The results align with experience factors identified by [19] and the modification of service-dominant logic for public service contexts discussed by [20]. The unique contribution of this integrated approach lies in its novel methodology and unique case application, as emphasized in [14]. Certain design requirements would have been overlooked without combining insights from different design traditions. The approach also incorporates systematic methods from PSS design, particularly regarding interactions between physical and non-physical elements [21]. However, the case study doesn’t con- form to traditional PSS categories [21–25], since it involves physical products where both ownership and functionality transfer completely to customers, without supplementary services or intangible alternatives. Rather, the case study exemplifies a complex service system due to its structure and composition, matching the classification system proposed by [26]. By combining perspectives and employing both systematic and participatory approaches, this research demonstrates the importance of interdisciplinary integration in enhancing service system development, providing an innovative solution to managing the complexities of contempo- rary service ecosystems. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. These proven applications highlight CBM’s potential in aviation maintenance, where early fault Processes 2025, 13, 666 5 of 54 detection and proactive interventions are critical for aircraft availability and safety. The ATSaaS framework integrates CBM principles by utilizing AI-driven predictive analytics and real-time sensor data to optimize maintenance scheduling and minimize disruptions. The fundamental objective of CBM is to minimize maintenance expenses by examining periodic or ongoing data about essential component performance [30]. CBM implementations vary from basic to sophisticated systems. Most industries adopt a version focusing

content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and can be evaluated by Based on the lower and upper limits, the error confidence interval limits as the and is updated with time. Thus, the safe train can be calculated by combining the HPL and error range of two the envelope confidence interval as (5) 5. State perception performance evaluation 5.1. RAMS architecture for railway signaling The performance indices of GNSS-based positioning are different from the requirements of railway systems, especially for the safety-related railway applications. With the EN50126 standard definition, the RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability & Safety) architecture is presented with different requirements to specific railway systems and functions. 1) Reliability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform a specified function under specified conditions and within a specified time period. The evaluation indicators with respect to the reliability include failure rate, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) and MUT (Mean Up Time). 2) Availability. It refers to the extent to which a product or a system is in a workable or usable state at any moment when the required external re- sources are met or when it starts to perform its tasks. The availability of a product is a composite representation of its reliability and maintainability. 3) Maintainability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform specified maintenance work under the specified conditions and within the specified time using the specified procedures and resources for maintenance. 4) Safety. It refers to the ability to not incur risks that may cause damage. The safety requirements of the GNSS autonomous positioning- based train control system include functional safety requirements and other safety requirements. Among them, functional safety requirements are described by SIL (Safety Integrity Level), and different SIL levels are defined by the THR (Tolerable Hazard Rate). For a novel train control system based on autonomous perception using GNSS, the RAMS indices also have to be considered in the design and operation phases. The relationship of RAMS indices in the railway domain is shown in Fig. 7. If the reliability and maintainability are met, and long-term maintenance and control are performed according to the environment in which the system is located, the safety and availability of the system can also be met. 51 =zxgkkf[(),()](

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: and quantitative analysis have been done. The same method is also proposed for the European Railway Traffic Management System (ERTMS) [96], where the authentication protocol of the Eurobalise subsystem is taken as the research object and the security hazards from attackers are converted as the hazards of Security Module. However, the cases of [95] and [96] do not consider the whole system but focus on the subsystem or equipment, which means there are difficulties to describe the effects of security on the safety from the view of the system. As a result, the systems- theoretic approach e.g. STPA is more effective to assess the safety and security [97,98], which can describe the goal of security efforts on controlling system vulnerabilities. As the system is modeled as a hierarchical structure with constraints on the component behavior from the higher level at the lower level in system theory, the detailed control me- chanisms and operation principle can be included. Therefore, the re- lationship between safety and security can be built, which is still a complicated work. However, there are not valuable works on the comprehensive assessment of safety and security for CBTC systems. As the systems-theoretic approach can deal with the state space explosion which is the limitation of the event chain method, it could be an ap- propriate way to model the relationship between safety and security which can efficiently perform the comprehensive assessment of safety and security. 5.2. Resilience as the assessment indicator systems, As there are not enough research about the cyber security of CBTC systems, it is a question to measure the security level. In the traditional assessment of CBTC the Reliability, Availability, Maintainability and Safety (RAMS) are the four significant indicators, where the statistics is applied as the qualitative measure, such as the failure probabilities, Mean Time to Failure (MTTF), Mean Time be- tween Failures (MTBF), two-dimension risk matrices (risk probabilities and risk consequences). Therefore, the traditional function assessment focuses on the large time scale, which cannot evaluate the effects caused by the temporal or sudden disruption. However, security events are often unexpected based on fully preparation. As a result, the sta- tistics indicators are not appropriate to describe the security con- sequences. Facing the new requirements for the assessment of CBTC systems especially the security issues, some new concepts, indicators or metrics should be proposed. Cybersecurity events can be treated as the disruption on the normal operation. Hence, resilience is proposed to perform the risk assessment to improve safety, security,

content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript.pdf

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: to guide service system development, as noted by [18]. The results align with experience factors identified by [19] and the modification of service-dominant logic for public service contexts discussed by [20]. The unique contribution of this integrated approach lies in its novel methodology and unique case application, as emphasized in [14]. Certain design requirements would have been overlooked without combining insights from different design traditions. The approach also incorporates systematic methods from PSS design, particularly regarding interactions between physical and non-physical elements [21]. However, the case study doesn’t con- form to traditional PSS categories [21–25], since it involves physical products where both ownership and functionality transfer completely to customers, without supplementary services or intangible alternatives. Rather, the case study exemplifies a complex service system due to its structure and composition, matching the classification system proposed by [26]. By combining perspectives and employing both systematic and participatory approaches, this research demonstrates the importance of interdisciplinary integration in enhancing service system development, providing an innovative solution to managing the complexities of contempo- rary service ecosystems. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. These proven applications highlight CBM’s potential in aviation maintenance, where early fault Processes 2025, 13, 666 5 of 54 detection and proactive interventions are critical for aircraft availability and safety. The ATSaaS framework integrates CBM principles by utilizing AI-driven predictive analytics and real-time sensor data to optimize maintenance scheduling and minimize disruptions. The fundamental objective of CBM is to minimize maintenance expenses by examining periodic or ongoing data about essential component performance [30]. CBM implementations vary from basic to sophisticated systems. Most industries adopt a version focusing

It considers require- For technical management, one of the key competencies is to fi nd the optimal maintenance stra- ments by company policy, classifi cation societies, charters and legislative regulations. For that, we tegy. Unplanned maintenance causes a signifi cant part of ship operation costs. Including spare part build up on the development of SCEDAS® and develop an interdisciplinary software tool. procurement, unscheduled or corrective maintenance account for up to 25 % of the total ship ope- ration costs (Drewry Maritime Research, 2015). Finding and implementing an optimal maintenance strategy is therefore a crucial task in technical management and has a great impact on the success of the entire ship management. The goal of maintenance management is to balance costs of preventive maintenance or of external maintenance services with costs of equipment failure. The challenge is therefore to fi nd appropriate maintenance intervals that prevent unplanned maintenance and costly downtime of equipment. Another problem is to distribute the workload evenly under consideration of the vessel’s voyage in order to effi ciently deploy human resources and to avoid rescheduling of jobs. Increasing require- ments by classifi cation societies, charters and compliance pressures add to the complexity. 2 73 MAINTENANCE STRATEGIES TREND: CONDITION BASED AND PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE Nonetheless, the trend is going towards condition based or even predictive maintenance because of advances in sensing and computing. Condition based maintenance is a form of preventive maintenance in which the maintenance intervals are dependent on the equipment’s condition. An essential task of maintenance management is to fi nd the optimal maintenance strategy. It It implies to continuingly sense the equipment’s condition (e.g. temperature, vibration, acoustic should keep the vessel at constant operating state and avoid costly unscheduled maintenance and emission) and automatically derive appropriate maintenance intervals from the sensor data, e.g. by downtime of equipment. However, it should also be cost-effi cient and keep unnecessary main- defi ning thresholds or characteristic patterns. In the aviation industry, condition based maintenan- tenance measures at a minimum. The key is therefore to take maintenance measures only when ce has already been successfully deployed for some time. they are necessary without risking costly equipment failure. But how is this possible? In general, maintenance strategies can be categorized into corrective, preventive and predictive Predictive maintenance takes condition based maintenance to a higher level. content.title: Studie Digitaler Zwilling Schiffbau Schiffsbetrieb 2023
content.abstract_or_snippet: bar machen�« für die traditionsreiche Branche mit seinen speziellen Anfor- derungen und Herausforderungen – wie Besonderheiten bei der Datenübertragung auf See, komplexer Stakeholder- und Eignerstruktur oder vergleichsweise langer Produktlebenszyk- len etc. – noch sehr jung. Die im Rahmen dieser Ausarbeitung ausgewerteten Interviews haben einen positiven Ausblick der Branche auf die Poten- tiale der Technologie des Digitalen Zwillings gegeben und es wurden auch für den Aftersales attraktive Kernthemen für die weitere Forschung identifiziert. Voraussetzung ist in diesem Kontext für die Beteiligten, wie bei allen unternehmerischen Entscheidungen, eine absehbare Amortisation der entspre- chenden Anfangsinvestition. Zentrale positive Effekte werden durch die zielgerichtete Nutzung von Datenströmen zur Optimierung des Schiffs- und Flottenmanagements gesehen, die bei konsequenter Umset- zung Flotteneffekte sichtbar und Lösungsansätze empirisch belegbar machen. Innovative Dienstleistungskonzepte, wie etwa das virtuelle Trai- ning von Fachpersonal oder die remoteunterstützte Wartung und Instandhaltung von schwer zugänglichen Systemen, bieten darüber hinaus Potentiale, um den Betrieb der maritimen Anlagen weiter zu optimieren und kostenintensive Reisen zu reduzieren. Nicht zuletzt wird nach Expertenmeinung der Digitale Zwilling im Rahmen der Umweltwende auch instrumental zur Ent- wicklung in Richtung einer nachhaltigen Schifffahrt beitragen können. Es haben sich jedoch auch Hemmfaktoren herauskristallisiert, die einem breiten Einsatz in der Branche entgegenstehen. Angefangen bei einer traditionsbewussten Mentalität vieler Branchenvertreter über eine nicht final geklärte Frage der Datenhoheit bis hin zur schleppenden Netzinfrastruktur bezüg- lich des Datenaustausches auf den Meeren und im Inland, verzögern als technologieunabhängige Faktoren eine breite Akzeptanz des Digitalen Zwillings. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass die teilweise in der vor

metadata.task_rationale: Liefert belastbare Begriffsabgrenzungen und ein Klassifikationsschema als Basis für den Vergleich.

content.title: Studie Digitaler Zwilling Schiffbau Schiffsbetrieb 2023
content.abstract_or_snippet: ext © Adobe Stock Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Erfolgsfaktoren im Bereich Aftersales-Service Einschätzung durch die Unternehmen N = 51 Servicequalität Optimales Bestandsmanagement Flächendeckendes Serviceangebot Angebote auf Kunden- segmente zuschneiden Geringe Logistikkosten g n u t u e d e B e g i t f n ü k u Z h c o h l e t t i m Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit Reaktions- und Lieferzeiten Minimale Ausfallzeit beim Kunden Produktdesign für Aftersales optimieren Nutzen transparent kommunizieren Modulares Aftersales-Angebot Schlankes Ersatzteilsortiment Sporadische Nachfrage nach Ersatzteilen & Services managen Integration der eigenen Zulieferer mittel hoch Handlungsbedarf Abbildung 5: Erfolgsfaktoren Aftersales [eigene Darstellung nach VDMA] der Produkte mit Datenverkehrsnetzen, die derzeit besonders auf Hoher See noch nicht ausreichend ausgebaut sind, um einen umfassenden Datentransfer ortsunabhängig zu gewährleisten [8]. Diese Handlungsfelder für die Digitalisierung des maritimen Service ergänzen herkömmliche produktbegleitende Dienst- leistungen wie Wartung und Instandhaltung, das Angebot von Ersatzteilen, die Betreuung des Kunden und die Durchführung von Schulungen. Trotz vielfältiger Chancen ist eine Fokussierung auf produkt- begleitende Dienstleistungen allein kein Patentrezept für einen langfristigen Erfolg. Über viele Branchen hinweg ist zu beobachten, dass die Bereitstellung von Aftersales-Services oft eine größere Komplexität innehat als die Herstellung der Primärprodukte selbst. Hinzu kommt, dass in vielen Unter- nehmen produktbegleitende Dienstleistungen eher historisch und aus der Notwendigkeit heraus gewachsen sind, Primär- produkte nach dem Verkauf mit Ersatzteilen und Wartung zu versorgen. 27 © Adobe Stock Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: to guide service system development, as noted by [18]. The results align with experience factors identified by [19] and the modification of service-dominant logic for public service contexts discussed by [20]. The unique contribution of this integrated approach lies in its novel methodology and unique case application, as emphasized in [14]. Certain design requirements would have been overlooked without combining insights from different design traditions. The approach also incorporates systematic methods from PSS design, particularly regarding interactions between physical and non-physical elements [21]. However, the case study doesn’t con- form to traditional PSS categories [21–25], since it involves physical products where both ownership and functionality transfer completely to customers, without supplementary services or intangible alternatives. Rather, the case study exemplifies a complex service system due to its structure and composition, matching the classification system proposed by [26]. By combining perspectives and employing both systematic and participatory approaches, this research demonstrates the importance of interdisciplinary integration in enhancing service system development, providing an innovative solution to managing the complexities of contempo- rary service ecosystems. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. These proven applications highlight CBM’s potential in aviation maintenance, where early fault Processes 2025, 13, 666 5 of 54 detection and proactive interventions are critical for aircraft availability and safety. The ATSaaS framework integrates CBM principles by utilizing AI-driven predictive analytics and real-time sensor data to optimize maintenance scheduling and minimize disruptions. The fundamental objective of CBM is to minimize maintenance expenses by examining periodic or ongoing data about essential component performance [30]. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. content.abstract_or_snippet: trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and can be evaluated by Based on the lower and upper limits, the error confidence interval limits as the and is updated with time. Thus, the safe train can be calculated by combining the HPL and error range of two the envelope confidence interval as (5) 5. State perception performance evaluation 5.1. RAMS architecture for railway signaling The performance indices of GNSS-based positioning are different from the requirements of railway systems, especially for the safety-related railway applications. With the EN50126 standard definition, the RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability & Safety) architecture is presented with different requirements to specific railway systems and functions. 1) Reliability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform a specified function under specified conditions and within a specified time period. The evaluation indicators with respect to the reliability include failure rate, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) and MUT (Mean Up Time). 2) Availability. It refers to the extent to which a product or a system is in a workable or usable state at any moment when the required external re- sources are met or when it starts to perform its tasks. The availability of a product is a composite representation of its reliability and maintainability. 3) Maintainability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform specified maintenance work under the specified conditions and within the specified time using the specified procedures and resources for maintenance. 4) Safety. It refers to the ability to not incur risks that may cause damage. The safety requirements of the GNSS autonomous positioning- based train control system include functional safety requirements and other safety requirements. Among them, functional safety requirements are described by SIL (Safety Integrity Level), and different SIL levels are defined by the THR (Tolerable Hazard Rate). For a novel train control system based on autonomous perception using GNSS, the RAMS indices also have to be considered in the design and operation phases. The relationship of RAMS indices in the railway domain is shown in Fig. 7. If the reliability and maintainability are met, and long-term maintenance and control are performed according to the environment in which the system is located, the safety and availability of the system can also be met. 51 =zxgkkf[(),()](

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: and quantitative analysis have been done. The same method is also proposed for the European Railway Traffic Management System (ERTMS) [96], where the authentication protocol of the Eurobalise subsystem is taken as the research object and the security hazards from attackers are converted as the hazards of Security Module. However, the cases of [95] and [96] do not consider the whole system but focus on the subsystem or equipment, which means there are difficulties to describe the effects of security on the safety from the view of the system. As a result, the systems- theoretic approach e.g. STPA is more effective to assess the safety and security [97,98], which can describe the goal of security efforts on controlling system vulnerabilities. As the system is modeled as a hierarchical structure with constraints on the component behavior from the higher level at the lower level in system theory, the detailed control me- chanisms and operation principle can be included. Therefore, the re- lationship between safety and security can be built, which is still a complicated work. However, there are not valuable works on the comprehensive assessment of safety and security for CBTC systems. As the systems-theoretic approach can deal with the state space explosion which is the limitation of the event chain method, it could be an ap- propriate way to model the relationship between safety and security which can efficiently perform the comprehensive assessment of safety and security. 5.2. Resilience as the assessment indicator systems, As there are not enough research about the cyber security of CBTC systems, it is a question to measure the security level. In the traditional assessment of CBTC the Reliability, Availability, Maintainability and Safety (RAMS) are the four significant indicators, where the statistics is applied as the qualitative measure, such as the failure probabilities, Mean Time to Failure (MTTF), Mean Time be- tween Failures (MTBF), two-dimension risk matrices (risk probabilities and risk consequences). Therefore, the traditional function assessment focuses on the large time scale, which cannot evaluate the effects caused by the temporal or sudden disruption. However, security events are often unexpected based on fully preparation. As a result, the sta- tistics indicators are not appropriate to describe the security con- sequences. Facing the new requirements for the assessment of CBTC systems especially the security issues, some new concepts, indicators or metrics should be proposed. Cybersecurity events can be treated as the disruption on the normal operation. Hence, resilience is proposed to perform the risk assessment to improve safety, security,

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript.pdf

content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

It considers require- For technical management, one of the key competencies is to fi nd the optimal maintenance stra- ments by company policy, classifi cation societies, charters and legislative regulations. For that, we tegy. Unplanned maintenance causes a signifi cant part of ship operation costs. Including spare part build up on the development of SCEDAS® and develop an interdisciplinary software tool. procurement, unscheduled or corrective maintenance account for up to 25 % of the total ship ope- ration costs (Drewry Maritime Research, 2015). Finding and implementing an optimal maintenance strategy is therefore a crucial task in technical management and has a great impact on the success of the entire ship management. The goal of maintenance management is to balance costs of preventive maintenance or of external maintenance services with costs of equipment failure. The challenge is therefore to fi nd appropriate maintenance intervals that prevent unplanned maintenance and costly downtime of equipment. Another problem is to distribute the workload evenly under consideration of the vessel’s voyage in order to effi ciently deploy human resources and to avoid rescheduling of jobs. Increasing require- ments by classifi cation societies, charters and compliance pressures add to the complexity. 2 73 MAINTENANCE STRATEGIES TREND: CONDITION BASED AND PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE Nonetheless, the trend is going towards condition based or even predictive maintenance because of advances in sensing and computing. Condition based maintenance is a form of preventive maintenance in which the maintenance intervals are dependent on the equipment’s condition. An essential task of maintenance management is to fi nd the optimal maintenance strategy. It It implies to continuingly sense the equipment’s condition (e.g. temperature, vibration, acoustic should keep the vessel at constant operating state and avoid costly unscheduled maintenance and emission) and automatically derive appropriate maintenance intervals from the sensor data, e.g. by downtime of equipment. However, it should also be cost-effi cient and keep unnecessary main- defi ning thresholds or characteristic patterns. In the aviation industry, condition based maintenan- tenance measures at a minimum. The key is therefore to take maintenance measures only when ce has already been successfully deployed for some time. they are necessary without risking costly equipment failure. But how is this possible? In general, maintenance strategies can be categorized into corrective, preventive and predictive Predictive maintenance takes condition based maintenance to a higher level. updated_tasks.search_intent: Definitionen und Taxonomien von CBM vs reaktiv/korrektiv und präventiv (time/use-based) Instandhaltung finden.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Liefert belastbare Begriffsabgrenzungen und ein Klassifikationsschema als Basis für den Vergleich.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Kernunterschiede in Prozessen/Methoden: Zustandsüberwachung, Diagnose, Prognose (PHM), Entscheidungslogik gegenüber traditionellen Methoden herausarbeiten.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Erarbeitet die methodischen Bausteine (Sensorik→Daten→Modelle→Maßnahme) als Differenzierungsmerkmale.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Quantitative Vergleichskennzahlen zwischen CBM und traditionellen Strategien (Kosten, Verfügbarkeit, Ausfallraten, MTBF/MTTR, Risiko) sammeln.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Stützt den Vergleich durch messbare Effekte und Benchmark-Ergebnisse aus Studien/Industrieanwendungen.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Maritime Leitlinien/Standards/Regelwerke zu CBM und zustandsbasierter Überwachung (IMO/ISM, IACS, Klassifikationsgesellschaften) identifizieren.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Verortet CBM im maritimen Compliance- und Regelwerkskontext und zeigt akzeptierte Praktiken.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

domain_applicability.reason: Nur Abkürzungsverzeichnis/Begriffe zu Predictive/Prescriptive Maintenance (AHM, HUMS, CBM); keine quantitativen Vergleichskennzahlen, Aviation-terminologisch.

It considers require- For technical management, one of the key competencies is to fi nd the optimal maintenance stra- ments by company policy, classifi cation societies, charters and legislative regulations. For that, we tegy. Unplanned maintenance causes a signifi cant part of ship operation costs. Including spare part build up on the development of SCEDAS® and develop an interdisciplinary software tool. procurement, unscheduled or corrective maintenance account for up to 25 % of the total ship ope- ration costs (Drewry Maritime Research, 2015). Finding and implementing an optimal maintenance strategy is therefore a crucial task in technical management and has a great impact on the success of the entire ship management. The goal of maintenance management is to balance costs of preventive maintenance or of external maintenance services with costs of equipment failure. The challenge is therefore to fi nd appropriate maintenance intervals that prevent unplanned maintenance and costly downtime of equipment. Another problem is to distribute the workload evenly under consideration of the vessel’s voyage in order to effi ciently deploy human resources and to avoid rescheduling of jobs. Increasing require- ments by classifi cation societies, charters and compliance pressures add to the complexity. 2 73 MAINTENANCE STRATEGIES TREND: CONDITION BASED AND PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE Nonetheless, the trend is going towards condition based or even predictive maintenance because of advances in sensing and computing. Condition based maintenance is a form of preventive maintenance in which the maintenance intervals are dependent on the equipment’s condition. An essential task of maintenance management is to fi nd the optimal maintenance strategy. It It implies to continuingly sense the equipment’s condition (e.g. temperature, vibration, acoustic should keep the vessel at constant operating state and avoid costly unscheduled maintenance and emission) and automatically derive appropriate maintenance intervals from the sensor data, e.g. by downtime of equipment. However, it should also be cost-effi cient and keep unnecessary main- defi ning thresholds or characteristic patterns. In the aviation industry, condition based maintenan- tenance measures at a minimum. The key is therefore to take maintenance measures only when ce has already been successfully deployed for some time. they are necessary without risking costly equipment failure. But how is this possible? In general, maintenance strategies can be categorized into corrective, preventive and predictive Predictive maintenance takes condition based maintenance to a higher level. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: to guide service system development, as noted by [18]. The results align with experience factors identified by [19] and the modification of service-dominant logic for public service contexts discussed by [20]. The unique contribution of this integrated approach lies in its novel methodology and unique case application, as emphasized in [14]. Certain design requirements would have been overlooked without combining insights from different design traditions. The approach also incorporates systematic methods from PSS design, particularly regarding interactions between physical and non-physical elements [21]. However, the case study doesn’t con- form to traditional PSS categories [21–25], since it involves physical products where both ownership and functionality transfer completely to customers, without supplementary services or intangible alternatives. Rather, the case study exemplifies a complex service system due to its structure and composition, matching the classification system proposed by [26]. By combining perspectives and employing both systematic and participatory approaches, this research demonstrates the importance of interdisciplinary integration in enhancing service system development, providing an innovative solution to managing the complexities of contempo- rary service ecosystems. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. These proven applications highlight CBM’s potential in aviation maintenance, where early fault Processes 2025, 13, 666 5 of 54 detection and proactive interventions are critical for aircraft availability and safety. The ATSaaS framework integrates CBM principles by utilizing AI-driven predictive analytics and real-time sensor data to optimize maintenance scheduling and minimize disruptions. The fundamental objective of CBM is to minimize maintenance expenses by examining periodic or ongoing data about essential component performance [30]. CBM implementations vary from basic to sophisticated systems. Most industries adopt a version focusing

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: to guide service system development, as noted by [18]. The results align with experience factors identified by [19] and the modification of service-dominant logic for public service contexts discussed by [20]. The unique contribution of this integrated approach lies in its novel methodology and unique case application, as emphasized in [14]. Certain design requirements would have been overlooked without combining insights from different design traditions. The approach also incorporates systematic methods from PSS design, particularly regarding interactions between physical and non-physical elements [21]. However, the case study doesn’t con- form to traditional PSS categories [21–25], since it involves physical products where both ownership and functionality transfer completely to customers, without supplementary services or intangible alternatives. Rather, the case study exemplifies a complex service system due to its structure and composition, matching the classification system proposed by [26]. By combining perspectives and employing both systematic and participatory approaches, this research demonstrates the importance of interdisciplinary integration in enhancing service system development, providing an innovative solution to managing the complexities of contempo- rary service ecosystems. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. These proven applications highlight CBM’s potential in aviation maintenance, where early fault Processes 2025, 13, 666 5 of 54 detection and proactive interventions are critical for aircraft availability and safety. The ATSaaS framework integrates CBM principles by utilizing AI-driven predictive analytics and real-time sensor data to optimize maintenance scheduling and minimize disruptions. The fundamental objective of CBM is to minimize maintenance expenses by examining periodic or ongoing data about essential component performance [30]. CBM implementations vary from basic to sophisticated systems. Most industries adopt a version focusing

RAMS architecture for railway signaling The performance indices of GNSS-based positioning are different from the requirements of railway systems, especially for the safety-related railway applications. With the EN50126 standard definition, the RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability & Safety) architecture is presented with different requirements to specific railway systems and functions. 1) Reliability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform a specified function under specified conditions and within a specified time period. The evaluation indicators with respect to the reliability include failure rate, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) and MUT (Mean Up Time). 2) Availability. It refers to the extent to which a product or a system is in a workable or usable state at any moment when the required external re- sources are met or when it starts to perform its tasks. The availability of a product is a composite representation of its reliability and maintainability. 3) Maintainability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform specified maintenance work under the specified conditions and within the specified time using the specified procedures and resources for maintenance. 4) Safety. It refers to the ability to not incur risks that may cause damage. The safety requirements of the GNSS autonomous positioning- based train control system include functional safety requirements and other safety requirements. Among them, functional safety requirements are described by SIL (Safety Integrity Level), and different SIL levels are defined by the THR (Tolerable Hazard Rate). For a novel train control system based on autonomous perception using GNSS, the RAMS indices also have to be considered in the design and operation phases. The relationship of RAMS indices in the railway domain is shown in Fig. 7. If the reliability and maintainability are met, and long-term maintenance and control are performed according to the environment in which the system is located, the safety and availability of the system can also be met. 51 =zxgkkf[(),()](

The same method is also proposed for the European Railway Traffic Management System (ERTMS) [96], where the authentication protocol of the Eurobalise subsystem is taken as the research object and the security hazards from attackers are converted as the hazards of Security Module. However, the cases of [95] and [96] do not consider the whole system but focus on the subsystem or equipment, which means there are difficulties to describe the effects of security on the safety from the view of the system. As a result, the systems- theoretic approach e.g. STPA is more effective to assess the safety and security [97,98], which can describe the goal of security efforts on controlling system vulnerabilities. As the system is modeled as a hierarchical structure with constraints on the component behavior from the higher level at the lower level in system theory, the detailed control me- chanisms and operation principle can be included. Therefore, the re- lationship between safety and security can be built, which is still a complicated work. However, there are not valuable works on the comprehensive assessment of safety and security for CBTC systems. As the systems-theoretic approach can deal with the state space explosion which is the limitation of the event chain method, it could be an ap- propriate way to model the relationship between safety and security which can efficiently perform the comprehensive assessment of safety and security. 5.2. Resilience as the assessment indicator systems, As there are not enough research about the cyber security of CBTC systems, it is a question to measure the security level. In the traditional assessment of CBTC the Reliability, Availability, Maintainability and Safety (RAMS) are the four significant indicators, where the statistics is applied as the qualitative measure, such as the failure probabilities, Mean Time to Failure (MTTF), Mean Time be- tween Failures (MTBF), two-dimension risk matrices (risk probabilities and risk consequences). Therefore, the traditional function assessment focuses on the large time scale, which cannot evaluate the effects caused by the temporal or sudden disruption. However, security events are often unexpected based on fully preparation. As a result, the sta- tistics indicators are not appropriate to describe the security con- sequences. Facing the new requirements for the assessment of CBTC systems especially the security issues, some new concepts, indicators or metrics should be proposed. Zentrale positive Effekte werden durch die zielgerichtete Nutzung von Datenströmen zur Optimierung des Schiffs- und Flottenmanagements gesehen, die bei konsequenter Umset- zung Flotteneffekte sichtbar und Lösungsansätze empirisch belegbar machen. Innovative Dienstleistungskonzepte, wie etwa das virtuelle Trai- ning von Fachpersonal oder die remoteunterstützte Wartung und Instandhaltung von schwer zugänglichen Systemen, bieten darüber hinaus Potentiale, um den Betrieb der maritimen Anlagen weiter zu optimieren und kostenintensive Reisen zu reduzieren. Die im Rahmen dieser Ausarbeitung ausgewerteten Interviews haben einen positiven Ausblick der Branche auf die Poten- tiale der Technologie des Digitalen Zwillings gegeben und es wurden auch für den Aftersales attraktive Kernthemen für die weitere Forschung identifiziert. Voraussetzung ist in diesem Kontext für die Beteiligten, wie bei allen unternehmerischen Entscheidungen, eine absehbare Amortisation der entspre- chenden Anfangsinvestition. Zentrale positive Effekte werden durch die zielgerichtete Nutzung von Datenströmen zur Optimierung des Schiffs- und Flottenmanagements gesehen, die bei konsequenter Umset- zung Flotteneffekte sichtbar und Lösungsansätze empirisch belegbar machen. Innovative Dienstleistungskonzepte, wie etwa das virtuelle Trai- ning von Fachpersonal oder die remoteunterstützte Wartung und Instandhaltung von schwer zugänglichen Systemen, bieten darüber hinaus Potentiale, um den Betrieb der maritimen Anlagen weiter zu optimieren und kostenintensive Reisen zu reduzieren. Nicht zuletzt wird nach Expertenmeinung der Digitale Zwilling im Rahmen der Umweltwende auch instrumental zur Ent- wicklung in Richtung einer nachhaltigen Schifffahrt beitragen können. Es haben sich jedoch auch Hemmfaktoren herauskristallisiert, die einem breiten Einsatz in der Branche entgegenstehen. Angefangen bei einer traditionsbewussten Mentalität vieler Branchenvertreter über eine nicht final geklärte Frage der Datenhoheit bis hin zur schleppenden Netzinfrastruktur bezüg- lich des Datenaustausches auf den Meeren und im Inland, verzögern als technologieunabhängige Faktoren eine breite Akzeptanz des Digitalen Zwillings. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass die teilweise in der vor

domain_applicability.reason: Redundanter Abschnitt; beschreibt Anforderungen/Herausforderungen, nicht Instandhaltungsstrategien.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: ext © Adobe Stock Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Erfolgsfaktoren im Bereich Aftersales-Service Einschätzung durch die Unternehmen N = 51 Servicequalität Optimales Bestandsmanagement Flächendeckendes Serviceangebot Angebote auf Kunden- segmente zuschneiden Geringe Logistikkosten g n u t u e d e B e g i t f n ü k u Z h c o h l e t t i m Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit Reaktions- und Lieferzeiten Minimale Ausfallzeit beim Kunden Produktdesign für Aftersales optimieren Nutzen transparent kommunizieren Modulares Aftersales-Angebot Schlankes Ersatzteilsortiment Sporadische Nachfrage nach Ersatzteilen & Services managen Integration der eigenen Zulieferer mittel hoch Handlungsbedarf Abbildung 5: Erfolgsfaktoren Aftersales [eigene Darstellung nach VDMA] der Produkte mit Datenverkehrsnetzen, die derzeit besonders auf Hoher See noch nicht ausreichend ausgebaut sind, um einen umfassenden Datentransfer ortsunabhängig zu gewährleisten [8]. Diese Handlungsfelder für die Digitalisierung des maritimen Service ergänzen herkömmliche produktbegleitende Dienst- leistungen wie Wartung und Instandhaltung, das Angebot von Ersatzteilen, die Betreuung des Kunden und die Durchführung von Schulungen. Trotz vielfältiger Chancen ist eine Fokussierung auf produkt- begleitende Dienstleistungen allein kein Patentrezept für einen langfristigen Erfolg. Über viele Branchen hinweg ist zu beobachten, dass die Bereitstellung von Aftersales-Services oft eine größere Komplexität innehat als die Herstellung der Primärprodukte selbst. Hinzu kommt, dass in vielen Unter- nehmen produktbegleitende Dienstleistungen eher historisch und aus der Notwendigkeit heraus gewachsen sind, Primär- produkte nach dem Verkauf mit Ersatzteilen und Wartung zu versorgen. 27 © Adobe Stock Digitale Zwillinge im maritimen Kontext

domain_applicability.reason: Liste von Erfolgsfaktoren im Aftersales (Servicequalität, Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit etc.), keine allgemeine Instandhaltungs-Taxonomie.

all_chunks.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: to guide service system development, as noted by [18]. The results align with experience factors identified by [19] and the modification of service-dominant logic for public service contexts discussed by [20]. The unique contribution of this integrated approach lies in its novel methodology and unique case application, as emphasized in [14]. Certain design requirements would have been overlooked without combining insights from different design traditions. The approach also incorporates systematic methods from PSS design, particularly regarding interactions between physical and non-physical elements [21]. However, the case study doesn’t con- form to traditional PSS categories [21–25], since it involves physical products where both ownership and functionality transfer completely to customers, without supplementary services or intangible alternatives. Rather, the case study exemplifies a complex service system due to its structure and composition, matching the classification system proposed by [26]. By combining perspectives and employing both systematic and participatory approaches, this research demonstrates the importance of interdisciplinary integration in enhancing service system development, providing an innovative solution to managing the complexities of contempo- rary service ecosystems. Condition-based maintenance (CBM) represents a maintenance strategy where repair and replacement decisions depend on the equipment’s present or anticipated state [27]. This methodology incorporates a program that suggests actions based on monitoring results and follows three key stages: gathering data, analyzing information, and determining maintenance actions [28]. CBM advances beyond standard predictive maintenance by implementing automated warning systems that activate before equipment fails, utilizing sophisticated multi-variable techniques to fulfill operational needs. This approach has been successfully adopted across several industries, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving operational reliability and cost efficiency. For instance, in the railway sector, CBM is used to monitor wheelset wear and track conditions, reducing unexpected failures and improving safety. In the energy industry, wind turbines use CBM to detect gearbox anomalies, allowing for proactive maintenance and extended asset lifespan. Similarly, in automotive manufacturing, CBM supports robotic assembly lines by predicting equipment degradation, minimizing downtime, and optimizing production efficiency [29]. These proven applications highlight CBM’s potential in aviation maintenance, where early fault Processes 2025, 13, 666 5 of 54 detection and proactive interventions are critical for aircraft availability and safety. The ATSaaS framework integrates CBM principles by utilizing AI-driven predictive analytics and real-time sensor data to optimize maintenance scheduling and minimize disruptions. The fundamental objective of CBM is to minimize maintenance expenses by examining periodic or ongoing data about essential component performance [30]. Die qualitativen Anforderungen dieser Klassen an Bandbreite, Paketlauftzeit (Delay) und Laufzeitschwankung (Jitter) sind in Tabelle 3-1 dargestellt. Die Einhaltung dieser QoS Parameter wird von den Klassen jeweils stark (++), normal (+) oder gar nicht (--) berücksichtigt. 47 Rodriguez de Lope / Hackbarth / Garcia / Plückebaum / Ilic (2008). 28 Diskussionsbeitrag Nr. 315 Tabelle 3-1: Modellierte Verkehrsklassen und ihre Sensibilität hinsichtlich QoS Parametern Verkehrsklasse Durchschnittliche Bandbreite Maximale Bandbreite Durchschnittliches Delay Echtzeit Streaming Garantierte Daten Best Effort ++ ++ ++ + ++ + -- -- ++ + + -- Quelle: Rodriguez de Lope / Hackbarth / Garcia / Plückebaum / Ilic (2008): S.3 Jitter ++ ++ -- -- Im Rahmen der TELRIC Modellierung wird die Kapazität bestimmt, die ein Nutzer zum Abruf eines Dienstes benötigt und die anteilig je Nutzer sowie aggregiert über alle Nut- zer bei den Netzelementen benötigt werden. Dabei wird zwischen vier Nutzergruppen unterschieden: Standard- und Premium-Privatkunden sowie Standard- und Premium- Geschäftskunden. preview.query: Wie unterscheidet sich die zustandsorientierte Instandhaltung (CBM)  von herkömmlichen Methoden?
preview.research_plan_reasoning: Die Forschungsfrage zielt auf eine begriffliche und methodische Abgrenzung von zustandsorientierter Instandhaltung (Condition-Based Maintenance, CBM) gegenüber herkömmlichen Instan…

input.query: Wie unterscheidet sich die zustandsorientierte Instandhaltung (CBM)  von herkömmlichen Methoden?
input.research_plan_reasoning: Die Forschungsfrage zielt auf eine begriffliche und methodische Abgrenzung von zustandsorientierter Instandhaltung (Condition-Based Maintenance, CBM) gegenüber herkömmlichen Instandhaltungsstrategien (reaktiv/korrektiv, präventiv/zeit- bzw. nutzungsbasiert, zustandsüberwacht). Für die maritime Domäne sind zusätzlich Klassifikationsgesellschaften, ISM/IMO-Rahmen sowie typische Schiffssysteme (Antrieb, Hilfsmaschinen, Rumpf/Struktur) relevant. Die Zerlegung fokussiert (1) definitorische Grundlagen, (2) Vergleichsrahmen/Taxonomien, (3) messbare Unterschiede (Daten, Sensorik, Prognostik, Entscheidungslogik) und (4) maritime Normen/Guidelines und Fallstudien.

research_aspects.description: Erarbeitet klare Definitionen von CBM und grenzt sie systematisch von korrektiver/reaktiver sowie präventiver (zeit-/nutzungsbasierter) Instandhaltung ab; bildet die Basis für einen konsistenten Vergleich.

Analysiert, wie CBM über Zustandsüberwachung, Diagnose und Prognose (PHM) zu wartungsentscheidungen führt und wie sich Datenflüsse, Entscheidungslogik und Workflows von herkömmlichen Methoden unterscheiden.

Vergleicht benötigte Messgrößen, Sensorik/Condition Monitoring, Datenqualität und Auswerteverfahren (Schwellenwerte, Modelle, ML) als zentrale technische Unterscheidungsmerkmale zwischen CBM und traditionellen Ansätzen.

Identifiziert geeignete quantitative Metriken (Kosten, Verfügbarkeit, Ausfallraten, MTBF/MTTR, Risiko) und Bewertungsansätze, um Unterschiede zwischen CBM und traditionellen Strategien messbar zu machen.

Berücksichtigt IMO/ISM, IACS/Klassifikationsgesellschaften sowie typische Schiffssysteme und Fallstudien, da diese Rahmenbedingungen und Praxisbeispiele die Umsetzung und Abgrenzung von CBM im maritimen Bereich prägen.
